text: After completing the abdominal surgical phase with total mesorectal excision, the surgeon proceeds to the perineal phase with the patient in a prone position. A lumbar liposuction is performed to obtain 100 cubic centimeters of fat. The perineal phase begins with a sphingal-shaped incision, bordered posteriorly by the last cortical vertebra, laterally by the ischial tuberosities, and anteriorly by the perineum. A blue dye spot is visible in the pre-sacral space, marking the lowest limit of mesorectal dissection. The surgeon sections the large cortisone vertebra at its joint and progresses to section the levator muscles near their origin. For specimen aversion, a gauze sutured to the colon during the abdominal phase is used to facilitate extraction and protect the mesorectum.

The surgeon places a biologic mesh to address the anatomic defect following an extralevator abdominal perineal dissection. The mesh is anchored to the coccyx and secured with interrupted stitches on the sides, with the final stitch placed in the anterior zone while carefully avoiding the urethra. Processed fat is injected into the closed subcutaneous tissue to fill the cavity and prevent evisceration and perineal hernia.
